coredata.dc:title: Fe(Ⅱ)-doped ZIF-67 derivatives-based composites as nanozyme for dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent detection of SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yue
Xie, Zihao
Pei, Fubin
Wu, Yi
Feng, Shasha
Hao, Qingli
Liu, Bing
Mu, Xihui
Lei, Wu
Tong, Zhaoyang
coredata.dc:description: The repeated outbreaks of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) have seriously threatened human health and economic development. Therefore, it is still significant to explore an efficient method for severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) detection. Herein, we designed a dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent immunosensor based on Fe2+ doped ZIF-67 derivatives-based composites, Pt nanoparticles loaded on CoFe nanoparticles embedded porous carbon-nitrogen hexahedron (CoFe/NC@Pt), for detecting SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein. The doping of Fe2+ generated bimetallic CoFe nanoparticles and formed more defects after pyrolysis. Meanwhile, the peroxides-like activity significantly improved owning to the synergistic effect of Co and Fe nanoparticles. Furthermore, loaded Pt nanoparticles not only provided more active sites, but also could couple antibodies by modifying functional groups. CoFe/NC@Pt exhibited good peroxidase-like activity and could catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxides in the presence of H2O2, generating visible color signals. While the oxTMB could quench the fluorescence of carbon dots through inner filter effect, thereby providing fluorescent signals. The limit of detection were 0.022 ng/mL (colorimetric) and 0.018 ng/mL (fluorescent). And the specificity of the immunosensor was verified by comparing with other interferents. Therefore, this dual-mode immunosensor with good performance provided a reliable strategy for diagnosis of COVID-19.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe2+ doped ZIF-67 derivatives
Peroxidase-like activity
Dual-mode detection
SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The steady-state kinetics and catalytic mechanism of CoFe/NC@Pt
Detection performance based on the dual-mode immunosensor
Dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent detection of SARS-CoV-2 virus

National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) CoFe-ZIFs-67, (B) CoFe/NC; (C-D) TEM images of CoFe/NC@Pt; Elemental mapping images of (E) C, (F) N, (G) Co, (H) Fe, (I) Pt and (J) overlap; (K) EDS image of CoFe/NC@Pt; (L) high-resolution TEM image of CoFe/NC@Pt; (M) XRD patterns of Co/NC, CoFe/NC, CoFe/NC@Pt and CoFe-ZIFs-67.
XPS spectra of CoFe/NC@Pt: (A) Full spectrum, (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, (D) Co 2p, (E) Fe 2p and (F) Pt 4 f.

(A) Raman spectrum of Co/NC@Pt and CoFe/NC@Pt; Steady-state kinetic assays of CoFe/NC@Pt: (B) The concentration of H2O2 was 10 mM and the TMB concentration was varied; (C) The concentration of TMB was 0.3 mM and the H2O2 concentration was varied; the insets show their corresponding Lineweaver-Burk curve (n = 3). (D) EPR spectra of DMPO+H2O2 +CoFe/NC@Pt and DMPO+H2O2.

(A) TEM image of CDs; (B) Fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of CDs, UV–vis absorption spectra of oxTMB; (C) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs with or without oxTMB; (D) zeta potential corresponding to MPA of different dosage.
(A) UV–vis spectrum and (B) corresponding linear relationship, (C) fluorescence spectrum and (D) corresponding linear relationship of N-protein with different concentrations (n = 5).
Selectivity of this dual-mode immunoassay for N-protein detection: (A) colorimetric channel, (B) fluorescent channel (n = 3).
Construction of colorimetric and fluorescent immunosensor based on CoFe/NC@Pt.

head.ce:title: Fe(Ⅱ)-doped ZIF-67 derivatives-based composites as nanozyme for dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent detection of SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, JiangSu 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, JiangSu, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The repeated outbreaks of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) have seriously threatened human health and economic development. Therefore, it is still significant to explore an efficient method for severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) detection. Herein, we designed a dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent immunosensor based on Fe2+ doped ZIF-67 derivatives-based composites, Pt nanoparticles loaded on CoFe nanoparticles embedded porous carbon-nitrogen hexahedron (CoFe/NC@Pt), for detecting SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein. The doping of Fe2+ generated bimetallic CoFe nanoparticles and formed more defects after pyrolysis. Meanwhile, the peroxides-like activity significantly improved owning to the synergistic effect of Co and Fe nanoparticles. Furthermore, loaded Pt nanoparticles not only provided more active sites, but also could couple antibodies by modifying functional groups. CoFe/NC@Pt exhibited good peroxidase-like activity and could catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxides in the presence of H2O2, generating visible color signals. While the oxTMB could quench the fluorescence of carbon dots through inner filter effect, thereby providing fluorescent signals. The limit of detection were 0.022 ng/mL (colorimetric) and 0.018 ng/mL (fluorescent). And the specificity of the immunosensor was verified by comparing with other interferents. Therefore, this dual-mode immunosensor with good performance provided a reliable strategy for diagnosis of COVID-19.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19), a severe acute respiratory disease that caused by SARS-CoV-2, has sparked a global pandemic, resulting in significant threats to public health and economic stability [1]. The virus is constantly undergoing mutations since the inherent mutability of its genome [2]. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) recently reported 12 variant strains of SARS-CoV-2, which poses challenges to testing strategies [3]. Therefore, detecting mutants with optimal performance is an urgent task. SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein (N-protein), as a structural protein, is the most expressed among these immunodominant proteins and exhibits high immunogenicity [4]. Furthermore, a substantial number of free N-protein are released into the host body during the early stages of virus replication [5]. Consequently, N-protein is a suitable antigenic target for COVID-19 diagnosis.

Compared with electrochemical detection, photothermal detection and other methods, enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is regarded as the most universal and powerful detection method in the clinical diagnosis of protein biomarkers because of the characters of simplicity, high throughput, and low cost [6–8]. However, the traditional colorimetric ELISA using horseradish peroxidase (HRP) that oxidize substrates to generate signals also has many drawbacks such as false positive results and low sensitivity [9]. Therefore, some approaches such as designing dual signal sensors and introducing nanozymes have been reported to improve these shortcomings. Fluorescent signal with the advantages of fast response and high sensitivity, are often used to construct dual-mode immunosensors [10]. Unlike single signal immunosensors, dual-mode immunosensors provide more reliable results as these signals are completely independent and the detection results can be mutually verified [11]. In addition, suitable detection methods can be selected based on different experimental conditions, which improved the flexibility of detection [12,13].

Natural enzymes embrace the features of high catalytic activity, selective specificity, and good biocompatibility [14], while the difficulties in preparation, purification, and storage have limited their application in clinical testing. Nanozymes have been constantly emerging as potential substitutes for nature enzymes owning to their merits of high stability, synthesis with low cost, and ease of modification [15]. Thus, using nanozymes to couple antibodies is an effective measure to improve the analytical performance of immunosensors [16,17]. To date, noble metal, metallic oxide, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and their derivatives have been developed as nanozymes for applications in sensors, cancer treatment, and catalysis [18,19]. Metal/nitrogen doped carbon porous (M/NC) obtained by high-temperature pyrolysis MOFs aroused widespread interest due to the outstanding conductivity, large specific surface area and abundant active site [20]. The recently reported Co/NC derived from ZIF-67 possessed excellent catalytic performance because of the synergistic effect between Co nanoparticles and NC [21]. In particular, studies have shown that doping of metal ions can effectively improve the catalytic activity of M/NC on account of the synergetic effect of different metal ions [22]. For example, Co and Fe can finely adjust their internal electron distribution and reduce the reaction energy barrier greatly in the catalytic process [23]. In addition, the porous structure formed after pyrolysis provides a channel for effective contact between the catalyst and substrate, which is beneficial for improving the reaction rate [24,25]. Pt nanoparticles (Pt NPs) have been confirmed to have excellent peroxidase-like activity. Loading Pt NPs on the surface of NC not only improves the catalytic performance of the composite material, but also couples with antibodies through functional group modification [26,27]. With these in mind, loading Pt NPs on the surface of CoFe/NC to improve the catalytic performance of nanozymes is a method worth exploring.

In this study, a dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent immunosensor based on CoFe/NC@Pt was fabricated to detect N-protein with high sensitivity. The CoFe/NC was prepared through pyrolysis of CoFe-ZIFs-67. Then Pt NPs were loaded on the surface of CoFe/NC via in-situ reduction method to form CoFe/NC@Pt. The synergistic effect of Co and Fe NPs enabled the formed CoFe nanoparticles (CoFe NPs) to exhibit enhanced peroxidase-like activity. Meanwhile, the doping of Fe2+ produced more defects, thereby generating more catalytical sites and accelerating electrical conductivity. In addition, the porous structure provided an effective pathway for catalytic reactions. Furthermore, Pt NPs loaded on the surface of CoFe/NC effectively exposed more catalytic active sites and could couple antibodies. In the presence of H2O2, CoFe/NC@Pt could catalyze the substrate TMB to produce blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) due to the excellent peroxidase-like activity of CoFe/NC@Pt. Subsequently, the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) could be quenched by oxTMB through inner filter effect (IFE). As shown in Scheme 1, the dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent immunosensor was constructed, and this immunosensor exhibited good analytical performance for detection of N-protein, which will provide a reliable method for accurate and rapid diagnosis of COVID-19.

9.08 g 2-MeIM was dissolved in 140 mL H2O, and then 50 μL TEA was added to form solution A under N2 atmosphere; 0.58 g Co(NO3)2·6 H2O, 0.11 g FeSO4·7 H2O and 0.02 g CTAB were dissolved in 40 mL H2O to form solution B under N2 atmosphere. Then, solution B was quickly poured into solution A and stirred for 1 h under N2 atmosphere. The purple powder was obtained by centrifugating and washing with anhydrous ethanol several times. Finally, the solid was vacuum-dried overnight.

Subsequently, CoFe/NC was obtained via a high-temperature pyrolysis method. Briefly, the purple powder was installed in the crucible and placed in the tubular furnace. The temperature increased at 2 ℃/min and maintained at 550 ℃ for 2 h, then cooled to room temperature under N2 atmosphere.

50 mg of CoFe/NC was dispersed in 50 mL of H2O through ultrasonic treatment for 20 min, and then 50 μL 1% HPtCl6 solution was added. The mixture was stirred for 1 h, and then 2 mL of NaBH4 solution (0.01%) was injected to continue stirring for 2 h. After centrifuging and washing with water three times, CoFe/NC@Pt was successfully prepared through vacuum drying overnight.

Details of synthesis of Co/NC, steady-tate kinetic assays of CoFe/NC@Pt, surface modification of CoFe/NC@Pt, preparation of CoFe/NC@Pt-Ab2 conjugates, construction of dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent immunosensor, immunoassay procedure based on traditional ELISA are listed in Supplementary material.

The morphology plays a crucial role in the properties of materials and the performance of sensors. According to the SEM images ( Fig. 1A), the prepared CoFe-ZIFs-67 presented a cubic structure with a smooth surface and the particle size was about 500 nm. Fig. 1B showed that the resulting CoFe/NC maintains its original skeleton, while its surface became rougher and presented a small amount of concave after pyrolysis in N2 atmosphere. Then, Pt nanoparticles (Pt NPs) were grown on its surface by in-situ reduction method. TEM images (Fig. 1C-D) further reviewed that CoFe/NC@Pt still retained a cubic structure. Moreover, CoFe/NC@Pt proved to possess good dispersion (Fig. S1A). The chemical composition of CoFe/NC@Pt was further characterized by element mapping analysis. The mapping images (Fig. 1E–J) showed that C, N, Co, Fe and Pt elements were evenly distributed. Meanwhile, the presence of elements of Co and Fe demonstrated CoFe NPs nanoparticles were formed after pyrolysis. In addition, the existence of Pt element indicated that Pt NPs loaded on the surface of CoFe/NC successfully. The EDS images (Fig. 1K) of CoFe/NC@Pt could further confirmed its elemental composition. As illustrated in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1L), the interlamellar distance of the lattice fringes with 0.343 nm was corresponding to the (002) crystal plans of graphite carbon. Additionally, the lattice distance of 0.205 nm was attributed to the (110) planes of CoFe NPs.

X-ray diffractometer (XRD) analysis also confirmed the formation of CoFe NPs. As displayed in Fig. 1M, the appearance of characteristic peaks indicated the successful preparation of CoFe-ZIFs-67. After high-temperature carbonization, the diffraction peak of CoFe-ZIFs-67 completely disappeared, and some new peaks were formed. The characteristic peaks located at 44.83° and 65.26° can be attributed the (110) and (200) facets of CoFe NPs (PDF#44–1433), respectively. Compared with the (111) faces of Co NPs, it can be found that the (110) facets of Co NPs have shifted, indicating CoFe NPs was successfully formed after pyrolysis. The results were matched with TEM results. In addition, the peak located at 26.4° correspond to the (002) facets of graphite carbon (PDF#41–1487). The results proved the formation of CoFe NPs in the N-doped porous carbon. Nevertheless, the diffraction peaks of Pt species could not be observed in the samples, which may be derived from the low content of Pt NPs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was further performed to verify the compositions and chemical valence states. With regard to the full XPS spectrum ( Fig. 2A), the materials were mainly composed of Co, Fe, O, N, C, and Pt elements, in good coincidence with the above results. As shown in Fig. 2B, the C 1 s spectrum could be separated into CC (284.1 eV), C-N (284.8 eV), C-O (286.3 eV), and O-CO (288.5 eV) [28], reflecting a large number of N atoms were successfully doped into the porous carbon. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2C) reviewed the existence of pyridine N, CoFe-N, pyrrole N, graphite N, and oxidized N at 392.6 eV, 399.2 eV, 400.3 eV, 401.3 eV, and 402.7 eV, respectively [29]. The presence of CoFe-N indicated the successful introduction of CoFe NPs into the NC. In addition, pyridinic N would adjust the local electronic structure to provide more active sites. Graphite N would accelerate electron transfer, thereby improving catalytic efficiency. The Co 2p spectrum was described in Fig. 2D, the three peaks located at 778.8 eV, 781.1 eV, and 782.6 eV corresponded to Co0, Co3+, and Co2+ for Co 2p3/2, respectively [30]. Similarly, the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 2E) located at 707.1 eV, 710.6 eV, and 712.5 eV were matched to Fe0, Fe2+, and Fe3+of Fe 2p3/2, respectively [31]. The appearance of Co2+, Co3+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ resulted from the surface oxidization of Co and Fe. The coexistence of Co and Fe proved the successful preparation of CoFe NPs, which was consistent with the above analysis. The characteristic peaks of 72.2 eV and 75.3 eV belonged to Pt 4 f7/2 and Pt 4 f5/2, respectively (Fig. 2F) [32]. The result indicated the successful loading of Pt NPs onto the surface of CoFe/NC.

The Raman spectrum was utilized to explore the graphitic degree of CoFe/NC@Pt. Fig. 3A displayed that the D-band located at 1320 cm−1 was associated to the structure defects and disorders of the carbon skeleton. The band of 1590 cm−1 was assigned to G-band, which related to the bond stretching of the pair of sp2 carbon atoms [33]. Therefore, the intensity ratio of D-band and G-band (ID/IG) represented the graphitization degrees of CoFe/NC@Pt. Fig. 3A also showed that the ID/IG values were 0.98 and 1.12 for the Co/NC@Pt and CoFe/NC@Pt, respectively. The results reflected that the degree of graphitization decreased and more defects were formed due to the doping of Fe2+, and it was beneficial for improving the catalytic activity. In addition, the catalytic activity of CoFe/NC was also investigated through HCl etching. As presented in Fig. S1D, the absorbance decreased after addition of HCl, and the reason for the decrease of catalytic activity was CoFe NPs on the surface of CoFe/NC were etched by HCl. The catalytic activity of Co/NC, CoFe/NC, and CoFe/ NC@Pt were also compared under the same conditions. The absorbance value of oxTMB corresponding to CoFe/NC@Pt was the strongest, reviewing an enhanced peroxidase-like activity though the synergistic effect between CoFe NPs and Pt NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The steady-state kinetics and catalytic mechanism of CoFe/NC@Pt
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of the CoFe/NC@Pt was evaluated by conducting the substrate TMB oxidation reaction in the presence of H2O2. As illustrated in Fig. S2A-B, CoFe/NC@Pt exhibited the highest peroxidase-like activity at pH 4 and temperature at 30 ℃, suggesting the catalytical conditions of CoFe/NC@Pt were similar to those of HRP. Meanwhile, TMB concentration and H2O2 concentration were also optimized. As shown in Fig. S2C-D, the optimized TMB and H2O2 concentration were 0.3 mM and 10 mM, respectively. Based on the optimal catalytic conditions, the peroxidase-like activity was further examined through steady-state kinetics analysis. Fig. 3B-C showed that the data of substrates TMB and H2O2 were fitted well with Michaelis-Menten curves and the curves were then converted to Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal plots [34]. The kinetic parameters were calculated and listed in Table S1. Compared with HRP, the Km value of CoFe/NC@Pt for substrate TMB was lower, indicating the affinity for TMB was better than that of HRP. Table S1 also displayed kinetic parameters of other nanozymes, and the Km values were not significantly different from that of other nanozymes. The result presented that the excellent peroxidase-like activity of CoFe/NC@Pt.

In addition, electron spin resonance (EPR) was used to explore the catalytic mechanism. The spin trap, 5,5-dimethyl pyridine N-oxide (DMPO), was chosen as the probe to detect hydroxyl radical (•OH). The DMPO could capture •OH with short lifespan and high reactivity to form DMPO-•OH spin adducts. Fig. 3D showed a characteristic spectrum of 1:2:2:1 intensity after the addition of CoFe/NC@Pt, which corresponded to the DMPO-•OH adducts [35]. The signal intensity of -•OH adduct was significantly higher than that of control group. Based on the above results, the catalytic process could be explained as following: H2O2 was first adsorbed by the CoFe/NC@Pt due to the porous structure. Then, •OH was produced under the synergetic catalysis of CoFe/NC@Pt. Finally, the generated •OH oxidized the substrate TMB to generate blue oxTMB.

To further verify the feasibility of this immunosensor, the characterization analysis on CDs was conducted. The TEM image ( Fig. 4A) exhibited that the CDs were spherical with uniform particle size. From Fig. 4B, it can be seen that the maximum excitation wavelength of CDs was 560 nm, and the emission wavelengths was 624 nm. The inset of Fig. 4B showed that the solution of CDs appeared orange, and the CDs emitted red fluorescence under laser irradiation. IFE is an effective fluorescence quenching mechanism that occurs when the absorption spectrum of the absorber in the fluorescence system overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorescent body, resulting in the absorption of the emitted light or excitation light by the absorber [36]. Fig. 4 C showed that the absorption spectrum of oxTMB overlapped with the emission spectrum of CDs, which was a prerequisite for the formation of IFE between CDs and oxTMB. As displayed in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was 1.405 ns and 1.368 ns before and after the addition of oxTMB. The slight change in fluorescence lifetime indicated that there was no energy resonance transfer between CDs and oxTMB. Based on the above analysis, the quenching mechanism could be inferred as the IFE process between CDs and oxTMB.

The amount of MPA would directly affect the number of -COOH groups on the surface of CoFe/NC@Pt, thereby affecting the number of conjugated antibodies (Ab2) and ultimately determining the sensitivity of the immunosensor. Fig. 4D demonstrated that the zeta potential of CoFe/NC@Pt decreased with the increase of the quantity of MPA and the value tended to be constant when the volume of MPA reached 80 μL. Therefore, 80 μL MPA was chosen for surface modification of CoFe/NC@Pt. The optimization of the blocking time, BSA concentration in the dispersion solution, concentration of capture antibody (Ab1), and CoFe/NC@Pt-Ab2 concentration are presented in Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performance based on the dual-mode immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: After optimizing the experimental conditions, the analytical performance of the dual-mode immunosensor was evaluated. As presented in Fig. 5 A, the absorbance of oxTMB increased gradually with the concentration of N-protein increased from 0.05 to 100 ng/mL, which indicated that higher concentrations of N-protein could induce the formation of more sandwiched immunocomplexes. Fig. 5B reflected a good linear correlation between the logarithmic concentration of N-protein (0.05–100 ng/mL) and the absorbance. The linear fitting equation was y = 0.180 + 0.063 *lgx (R2 =0.993), and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated as 0.022 ng/mL. Fig. 5 C showed that the fluorescent intensity of CDs gradually decreased as the concentration of N-protein increasing. As exhibited in Fig. 5D, the linear correlation between the logarithmic concentration of N-protein (0.05–100 ng/mL) and the fluorescent intensity were constructed as y = 9904.06–573.439 *lgx (R2 =0.991), and the LOD was calculated to be 0.018 ng/mL.

Furthermore, the specificity of the dual-mode immunosensor was assessed by testing several proteins (N-protein, S-protein, SARS N-protein, MERS N-protein, Flu A N-protein, and RSV glycoprotein). According to Fig. 6A-B, both the colorimetric and fluorescent channels exhibited relatively low signal. Even the concentrations of interfering proteins were 10 times higher than that of N-protein, the signal intensity was similar to control group. These results indicated that these interfering proteins could not form sandwich immunocomplexes to output detection signals, which was consistent with the specific binding of antibodies to their corresponding antigens. Therefore, the dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent immunosensor based on CoFe/NC@Pt represented satisfactory specificity for detecting N-protein.

The possibility of the immunosensor in practical applications was investigated by using the standard addition method to detect N-protein in artificial saliva and human serum. First, the artificial saliva and human serum were diluted 10 times with PBS containing 1% BSA. Thereafter, 2 μL, 10 μL, and 20 μL of 500 ng/mL N-protein were added to 1 mL of artificial saliva and human serum. The obtained spiked samples were 1 ng/mL, 5 ng/mL, and 10 ng/mL, respectively. Table S3 displayed that the recovery of colorimetric channel and fluorescent channel were 94.8%− 113.5% and 93.5%− 114.2% for artificial saliva and human serum, respectively. These results indicated that the immunosensor possessed high reliability for the detection of N-protein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent detection of SARS-CoV-2 virus
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the actual sample detection capability of the dual-mode immunosensor, the inactivated SARS-CoV-2 virus was lysed with lysis buffer and diluted 10 times, 100 times and 1000 times, respectively. The negative samples were collected from salal swabs of healthy individuals. In addition, the positive and negative samples were confirmed by commercial colloidal gold test strips. As displayed in Fig. S5A, the absorbance gradually decreased as the dilution ratio of inactivated viruses increased. However, all of the absorbances of COVID-19 samples were higher than that of healthy samples. Similarly, the fluorescent signals in Figure. S5B also changed accordingly. These results were consistent with the results of commercial colloidal gold test strips (Fig. S5C-D), indicating that the dual-mode immunosensor could accurately detect SARS-CoV-2 N-protein.

In this study, a dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent immunosensor was developed for the sensitive detection of N-protein by using CoFe/NC@Pt as labels. The CoFe/NC@Pt were prepared by pyrolysis and in-situ reduction method. The synergistic effect between Co and Fe NPs endowed the prepared CoFe/NC@Pt exhibited enhanced peroxidase-like activity. Besides, the loaded Pt NPs provided more active sites for catalytic reaction. On the basis of the peroxidase-like activity of CoFe/NC@Pt and the IFE between oxTMB and CDs, the constructed dual-mode immunosensor possessed higher detection accuracy due to the mutual verification of colorimetric and fluorescent signals compared with single-mode immunosensors. The immunosensor exhibited high sensitivity with LOD at 0.022 ng/mL for colorimetric signal and 0.018 ng/mL for fluorescent signal. Importantly, the feasibility analysis demonstrated the dual-mode immunosensor possess good practicability. Based on these results, we believe that the immunosensor will exert a positive effect on the rapid and accurate detection of N-protein.

Yue Sun: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing original-draft. Zihao Xie: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing original-draft. Fubin Pei: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing-review & editing. Yi Wu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing-review & editing. Shasha Feng: Formal analysis, Methodology. Qingli Hao: Supervision, Writing-review & editing. Bing Liu: Supervision, Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xihui Mu: Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation. Zhaoyang Tong: Methodology, Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition. Wu Lei: Methodology, Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Foundation of State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian (No. SKLNBC2022-06) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51972173).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134428.

Lancet

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nucleocapsid specific diagnostics for the detection of divergent SARS-CoV-2 variants
Front Immunol.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein is dynamic, disordered, and phase separates with RNA
Nat. Commun.

Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Li, H. Fu, X. Chen, P. Gong, G. Chen, L. Xia, et al., Facile and Sensitive Fluorescence Sensing of Alkaline Phosphatase Activity with Photoluminescent Carbon Dots Based on Inner Filter Effect, Anal Chem 88 (2016) 2720–2726. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.5b04193.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yue Sun is a M. A. graduated from Nanjing University of Science and Technology. She started her research in 2020. Her current research focuses on nanozyme-based immunosensors.
Zihao Xie is a M. A. graduated from Nanjing University of Science and Technology. He started his research in 2020. His current research focuses on lateral flow immunoassay.

Fubin Pei is a PhD candidate in the school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology. He started his research in 2020. His current research focuses on photoelectrochemical immunosensors.

Yi Wu is a PhD candidate in the school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology. She started her research in 2019. Her current research focuses on nanozyme-based sensors.

Shasha Feng is a PhD candidate in the school of chemistry and chemical engineering of Nanjing University of Technology. She started her research in 2018. Her current research focus is photoelectrochemical immunosensor.

Qingli Hao is a professor at Nanjing University Science and of Technology. She is the editorial member of Microchimica Acta. Her research interest is focused on functional nanomaterials electrochemistry, especially on inorganic nanomaterials composites with conducting polymers, carbon for electrochemical energy storage and conversion systems and electrochemical sensors.

Bing Liu is an associate professor in the State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian. His Ph.D. degree in applied chemistry in 2013 received. His current research focused on chemical and biological sensing.

Xihui Mu is an associate professor in the State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian. Her Ph.D. degree received in applied chemistry in 2016. Her current research focused on the preparation of functional magnetic nanomaterials, nanotechnology, design of new sensors, biological sensing.

Wu Lei (corresponding author) is a professor and doctoral supervisor at Nanjing University of Science and Technology. His research focuses on the fields of applied chemistry and electrochemistry, especially on electrochemical energy storage and conversion systems and photo/electrochemical sensors.

Zhaoyang Tong (corresponding author) is a professor in the State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian. His research work has been mainly focused on biochemistry, nanotechnology, chemical and biological sensing.
